    Dynamical Relaxation in Star Clusters

    Dynamical Relaxation

    Type:Intermediate concept
    Timescale:Few free-fall times (tff) for fractal erasure; longer for full relaxation
    Related: Fractal structure of star clusters
    Q parameter (fractality)
    Star cluster formation
    Gravity
    Subcluster formation

    Dynamical relaxation in star clusters refers to the gradual smoothing of a cluster's spatial and velocity structure through gravitational interactions among stars. For newly formed clusters, the most observationally relevant consequence is the erasure of the initial fractal substructure inherited from the parent molecular cloud — a transition from a hierarchically clumped distribution toward a smooth, centrally concentrated one.

    Fractal Erasure Timescale

    Simulations consistently find that the transition from fractal (low Q) to centrally concentrated (high Q) structure occurs over a few free-fall times:

    • Akhmetali et al. (2026)1: ~2.5 tff for 13 simulations of a 2513 M☉ centrally concentrated cloud, independent of resolution and initial fractality.
    • Laverde-Villarreal et al. (2025)2: ~2.5 tff for clouds of 2 × 104, 8 × 104, and 3.2 × 105 M☉ — consistent across an order of magnitude in mass.
    • Maschberger et al. (2010)3: low Q rises to non-fractal values within a couple of free-fall times in a gravitationally bound cluster.

    The consistency across cloud masses and resolutions suggests that this timescale is primarily set by the global free-fall time of the parent cloud, rather than local properties.1

    Prolonged Fractality via Subclusters

    Dynamical relaxation can be countered by the formation of new subclusters, either through stellar feedback-triggered compression or through the repeated merger and re-fragmentation of subcluster systems. In such cases, the Q parameter can remain below the fractal threshold beyond 4 tff, with Q oscillating as subclusters merge (Q rises) and re-separate (Q falls).1

    Two-Body Relaxation

    On longer timescales, two-body (star-star) gravitational scattering drives energy equipartition among stars and leads to mass segregation (heavy stars sink to the center) and eventual evaporation of the lightest stars from the cluster. The two-body relaxation timescale scales as trelax ~ N / ln N × tcross, where N is the number of stars and tcross is the crossing time — typically much longer than the initial fractal-erasure timescale.

    See Also

    • Fractal structure of star clusters
    • Q parameter (fractality)
    • Subcluster formation
    • Star cluster formation

    References
    1. Akhmetali, A. et al. (2026). "Evolution of fractality in centrally concentrated young clusters." A&A (submitted). arXiv:2603.16183
    2. Laverde-Villarreal, E. et al. (2025). "The Evolution of Substructure during Star Cluster Assembly." ApJ, 989, 22.
    3. Maschberger, Th. et al. (2010). "Properties of hierarchically forming star clusters." MNRAS, 404, 1061–1080.
    4. Daffern-Powell, E. C. & Parker, R. J. (2020). "Dynamical evolution of fractal structures in star-forming regions." MNRAS, 493, 4925–4935.
    5. Portegies Zwart, S. F. et al. (2010). "Young massive star clusters." ARA&A, 48, 431–493.
